metric_value: Prices for x450 begin at $25,999 for single-processor; most customers pay ~$38,000 for setup with two 1GHz Itanium 2 processors with 3MB cache
Larger 16-processor x450 sibling using Madison Itanium 2 6M and second-gen EXA due by end of year
There's no doubt that Itanium machines will begin to take material market share away from other architectures going forward this year
notes: Kastner verbatim — partially validated; Itanium took share at high end but Xeon dominated

metric_value: Xeon-based systems likely will bring in more revenue for at least the next five years
notes: Validated — Xeon revenue stayed >10x Itanium through 2008

metric_name: IBM strategic timing forced by Opteron announcement
metric_value: IBM had to release its Itanium product soon, given the plans it announced last week to sell some servers using AMD's Opteron processor. IBM had to have a strong Intel 32-bit and 64-bit product line in order not to confuse customers into thinking that there's some unannounced transition to AMD in the cards.
IBM's Itanium support trails that of rival Hewlett-Packard, which is betting the company on Itanium
notes: Kastner verbatim — historic phrasing of HP-Itanium risk

metric_name: Itanium accepted into IBM 4-line server portfolio
metric_value: Itanium acceptance means IBM contends with four server processor families: Xeon/Itanium (xSeries), Power4 (pSeries/iSeries), proprietary z (zSeries)
notes: IBM Karl Advani: too much simplicity also a problem

metric_value: Digital Equipment in the 1980s and Sun (Microsystems) in the 1990s showed that one-size-fits-all model is not sustainable
notes: Advani comment cited as DEC/Sun analogue

metric_value: IBM previously sold a server with first-gen Itanium ('Merced') which arrived late and performed poorly; only positioned for developer rebuilds, not real-world use
Itanium's debut hampered by inability to run older Pentium/Xeon software effectively; Intel addressing with new emulation software
notes: Persistent Itanium liability cited by Shankland
